videos.title: Never Mix Up The US and Canada Again
How to tell USA & Canada apart

hints.text: Speed signs say **'MAXIMUM'** in **km/h** — numbers higher than US ones, readable from afar; 'MAXIMUM' fits one line ('SPEED LIMIT' takes two)

shared_with.note: US signs say 'SPEED LIMIT' in miles, often ending in 5.

hints.text: Signs **less wordy** than the US: railroad crossbucks & yield signs typically **blank**
**British spelling** (centre, colour) + **metric system**
**Diamond-shaped checkerboard T-intersection signs** — the only country in the world with these
Usually **single yellow centre line** (double yellow decently common though)

shared_with.note: Double yellow is more common in the US.
shared_with.diff: Not 100% reliable — combine with other clues.

hints.text: Fair amount of **Gen2** (esp. rural/remote): big circular blur + halo-like sky distortion; Gen3 much more common than in the US, side-mirror blur = **two peaks** (US: single peak)

shared_with.note: Gen2 is very rare in the US (except Alaska/Hawaii).
shared_with.diff: North American Gen2 → lean Canada; two-peak mirror → Canada.

hints.text: **Communal mailboxes** (very rare in the US); Canada Post logo is **red** (USPS = blue)

shared_with.note: USPS blue logo; hardly any communal boxes.

hints.text: **North America vs Europe tells** (US + Canada): post mailboxes w/ flag; wooden poles + cylindrical metal transformers (never Europe); raised single insulator; short plates w/o blue strip; overhead stoplight bars; holed/U-profile signposts; tall water towers w/ town names; Gen4 smallcam w/ right mirror visible

Each province = own **plate design** (some readable blurred); several provinces don't require **front plates** (NB dropped them 2019 but older coverage still shows them; NL commercial vehicles keep front plates)
**French majority in Québec + parts of New Brunswick**; federal bilingualism → occasional bilingual signs anywhere (French communities also in NS/ON/MB)
Each province has its own **highway shield**; the green **Trans-Canada** shield appears in all 10 provinces; **km markers** vary by province — Ontario's have a unique **white square at the bottom**
Speed-sign variants: Ontario = black bottom section w/ 'km/h' or 'BEGINS'; BC = 'km/h' without black section; Québec sometimes French text
**Pole & transformer metas**:
bullets: Transformer faces the road → BC & New Brunswick (elsewhere parallel to wires)
Transformer at 45° → PEI & Nova Scotia (PEI: red numbers on the side)
Insulators facing to/away from road → QC, NS, PEI (elsewhere parallel; avoid intersections)
Ontario poletop: 2 side insulators w/ distinct attachment + 1 on top (stackable)
Québec: insulator rods angled slightly up; vertical black-yellow 'Q' label
Saskatchewan: insulators ALTERNATE 1-left/2-right ↔ 2-left/1-right (rarely MB)
Manitoba: two hanging insulators
PEI: small white pole sticker

hints.text: **Road-line metas**: double yellow in the East → most common Nova Scotia, then QC/PEI (Ontario prefers single); SK sometimes has **white dashes between the yellows**; centre rumble strips → AB & BC
Cones & chevrons: Ontario = **orange-black striped cones**; Québec = **orange-white cones** + the only province w/ **red-white chevrons** (rest of Canada: yellow-black)

3-digit **area codes** by region (7-digit numbers omit them); **provincial flags** appear often (Québec's Fleurdelisé especially); **Acadian flag** (French tricolour + yellow star) → N/E New Brunswick + pockets of NS/PEI
**Landscape regions**:
bullets: High mountains only in BC, western Alberta, Yukon
Yukon: boreal hills, conifers shrinking northward, lots of Gen2
BC interior (Kamloops/Kelowna): arid brown grasslands + pine hillsides
Great Plains: flat farmland, few trees, driest toward the Montana border
Canola (bright yellow fields): central-southern prairies (also North Dakota!)
NW Québec (Rouyn-Noranda): N-S grid, fields between forests, thin birches
S Saskatchewan: barren hilly river valleys (rest of the prairies = flat)
Aspens (white birch-like trunks, bright yellow in fall): MB/SK/AB forests
Canadian Shield (east): exposed rock everywhere
Québec City–Windsor corridor: densest population, flat green patchwork (looks like the US Great Lakes)
PEI: distinct RED soil + potato farms (occasionally NB/NS)
Newfoundland: Christmas-tree-like balsam firs

hints.text: **Bollard**: cylindrical black & white w/ 2 thin black bands — very common at intersections; band between can be yellow, rarely blue (Calgary) or green (Edmonton)
hints.uniq_note: NWT uses the same shape with THICKER bands; BC's version is thinner with wider band spacing.

hints.text: Rural roads numbered **sequentially in a grid**: Twp Rd = E–W (numbers rise northward), Rge Rd = N–S (rise westward, reset a few times) → near-exact location from one sign (similar naming rarely in SK); rare wire guardrails (posts + 3–4 metal wires) on major highways

Thin, slightly curved black-white **bollard** (esp. Vancouver Island); **concrete barriers** instead of guardrails; signs can have **green backsides** (also PEI); signs sometimes hung from unique posts
Bollards black & grey, usually w/ an **orange stripe below the grey**; wooden rectangular version further north; plain black-top version → MB & SK; **green-white striped signpost sticker**
Rural **grid roads named by number + cardinal**: 'Road 105N' = 105 miles north of the US border; E/W numbers count from the principal meridian just west of Winnipeg
Bollard cylindrical w/ 2 **thick** black bands (Alberta's are thinner)
**Diamond roadside markers** (colours vary); guardrails of cylindrical wooden posts + exactly **3 metal wires** (some posts w/ white reflective band)
**'Arrêt' stop signs = Québec only**; 'Stop/Arrêt' bilingual → most commonly New Brunswick (NB street signs bilingual too); QC T-intersection signs lack the checkerboard pattern

Long thin white **bollards attached to guardrails** w/ red or green top reflector; **yellow fire-hydrant markers** on signposts (Québec City: blue rectangle; Trois-Rivières: small; beware Edmundston NB); rectangular roadside markers (rarely NB divided highways)
**White triangles on signposts** (count varies)
Rectangular **lampheads** (also NS); Nova Scotia lamp arms = short & curved; flat **yellow bollards** on the NB Trans-Canada Highway
Guardrail posts w/ **pyramid-shaped tops**; diamond markers next to fire hydrants; **wooden sign boxes**; octagonal wood-plank bins on driveways; T-intersection signs w/ a yellow rectangular border
**Road spotlights**:
bullets: Dempster Hwy (YT-5/NWT-8 → Inuvik): unpaved Gen2, dark soil, tundra w/ short trees; flat + cloudier near Inuvik
Top of the World Hwy (W of Dawson City): overcast Gen2 high in the mountains
Hwy 1 near Beaver Creek (Yukon): group of motorcyclists ahead of the car
AB-93 (Banff/Jasper): steep sharp-peaked mountains both sides + thin tall trees
Drumheller (AB): treeless canyon-like rock formations
Trans-Labrador Hwy (500/510): sunny Gen4, thin pines, remote northern feel, low sun; snow near Happy Valley-Goose Bay
QC-167/QC-113: overcast Gen3 autumn, often snow + a lens smudge
Gaspé Peninsula inland: dense forested mountains, overcast
Cape Breton Highlands (NE NS): unusually much elevation, mixed forest
NL Route 430: SSW-NNE, washed-out lines, coast to the west, balsam firs + distant mountain wall east

hints.text: **Remote towns & trekkers**:
bullets: Iqaluit (NU capital): urban, turquoise bilingual street signs, bilingual speed signs
Churchill (N-MB): trekker w/ thin antenna front-right + unique street signs
Tulita (NWT): unique Google car + dark soil
Sanikiluaq (Hudson Bay): snowmobile, driver in dark brown coat visible
Inukjuak (N-QC): snowmobile visible through blur, NO driver (driver visible = Sanikiluaq)
Grise Fiord: northernmost covered town — unique mountains + ice floes
Resolute: barren w/ snow patches; Cambridge Bay: only NU Gen2 trekker
Qikiqtarjuaq: distinct mountains + red-tinted soil
Kangiqsualujjuaq (N-QC): green rocky hills, trees in the distance
Nain (Labrador): colourful wooden houses, big hill north, cliffs south, kids beside the trekker
Red Bay (S-Labrador): coast always visible, slightly red soil, white houses
Ellesmere Island: the world's northernmost coverage — glacier in barren rock (appears in competitive maps!)
